With a neat diagram, describe the basic structure of a finite automaton. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Tabulate the differences between the four types of automata in the Chomsky Hierarchy (FA, PDA, LBA, TM) based on: memory type, language recognized, grammar type, and an example application. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Central Concepts of Automata Theory** *(3 marks)*: - Automata Theory studies *abstract machines* and the *computational problems* they can solve. - The three central objects are: **Alphabet** ($\Sigma$), **Strings** ($w \in \Sigma^*$), and **Languages** ($L \subseteq \Sigma^*$). - An automaton is formally a 5-tuple $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where: - $Q$: finite set of states - $\Sigma$: input alphabet - $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$: transition function - $q_0 \in Q$: initial state - $F \subseteq Q$: set of accepting states **Basic Structure of a Finite Automaton** *(4 marks)*: - A finite automaton consists of an **input tape** (holding the string $w$), a **read head** (scans one symbol at a time), and a **control unit** (contains the states and transition logic). - The **current state** is updated via the transition function $\delta(q, a) = q'$. - When the input is fully consumed, the final state is checked: if it belongs to $F$, the string is **accepted**; otherwise, it is **rejected**. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual schematic. 1 mark is reserved for the diagram, 3 marks for the explanation.)* **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:** | Parameter | Finite Automaton (FA) | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) | Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) | Turing Machine (TM) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Memory Type** | None (only current state) | One Stack | Bounded Tape (linear in input size) | Unbounded Tape | | **Language Recognized** | Regular Languages | Context-Free Languages | Context-Sensitive Languages | Recursively Enumerable Languages | | **Grammar Type (Chomsky)** | Type-3 (Regular Grammar) | Type-2 (Context-Free Grammar) | Type-1 (Context-Sensitive Grammar) | Type-0 (Unrestricted Grammar) | | **Example Application** | Lexical Analyzer in Compilers, Vending Machines | Syntax Analyzer (Parser) in Compilers, XML Validation | Natural Language Sentence Analysis | General Algorithm Execution, Computability Studies | | **Power Level** | Least Powerful | Moderate | High | Most Powerful | *(1.5 marks per row — ½ mark for memory, ½ mark for language, ½ mark for grammar. The application column may be combined for partial credit.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing $\Sigma^*$ and $\Sigma^+$:** Many students forget that $\Sigma^+$ *excludes* the empty string $\varepsilon$. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. **Lose 1 mark** if you write $\Sigma^+ = \Sigma^*$. > > 2. **Forgetting the Closure Properties:** In questions on language operations, students often claim context-free languages are closed under intersection — they are **NOT**. Always quote a counter-example (e.g., $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m\}$, $L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n\}$). **Lose 2 marks** for a false closure claim. > > 3. **Incomplete 5-tuple Definition:** When defining an automaton, students often omit $F$ (accepting states) or confuse $q_0$ with $F$. The order **$(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$** must be strictly followed. **Lose 0.5 mark** per missing component. > > 4. **Mixing Up Concatenation Order:** $(uv)^R = v^R u^R$, not $u^R v^R$. This is a very common error. **Lose 1 mark** if the order is wrong. > > 5. **Arithmetic Error in $\vert \Sigma \vert^k$:** When $\vert \Sigma \vert = 2$ and $k = 4$, the answer is $16$, not $8$. Double-check exponentiation. **Lose 1 mark** for a wrong numerical answer. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):** > > - ☐ **Automaton = Self-Acting Machine** — a 5-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. > - ☐ **Alphabet $\Sigma$** is *finite* and *non-empty*. > - ☐ **String $w$** is a finite sequence of symbols from $\Sigma$. > - ☐ **Length $\vert w \vert$** counts symbols; $\vert \varepsilon \vert = 0$. > - ☐ **$\Sigma^k$** = set of all strings of length exactly $k$; $\vert \Sigma^k \vert = \vert \Sigma \vert^k$. > - ☐ **$\Sigma^*$** = all strings (including $\varepsilon$); $\Sigma^+$ = all strings *excluding* $\varepsilon$. > - ☐ **Language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$** is a *subset* of $\Sigma^*$. > - ☐ **Chomsky Hierarchy:** Type-0 ⊇ Type-1 ⊇ Type-2 ⊇ Type-3 (most powerful to least). > - ☐ **Automaton Classes:** TM > LBA > PDA > FA in terms of computational power. > - ☐ **Closure:** Regular languages are closed under Union, Intersection, Complement, Concatenation, and Kleene Star. > - ☐ **Non-Closure:** Context-free languages are NOT closed under Intersection and Complement. > - ☐ **Concatenation property:** $\vert uv \vert = \vert u \vert + \vert v \vert$. > - ☐ **Reverse property:** $(uv)^R = v^R u^R$. > - ☐ **Countability:** $\Sigma^*$ is countably infinite; the *set of all languages* over $\Sigma$ is uncountable. > - ☐ **Practical applications:** Compilers, NLP, Digital Circuits, Pattern Matching, AI Search, DNA Sequencing.

topics.title: Deterministic and Non-deterministic Finite Automata (DFA and NFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT524 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deterministic and Non-deterministic Finite Automata (DFA and NFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Alphabet, Strings, and Languages (Foundational Recap)

> [!NOTE]
> Before defining finite automata, we must fix the building blocks used in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

- **Alphabet ($\Sigma$)**: A **finite, non-empty** set of symbols. Example: $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$.
- **String (Word)**: A finite sequence of symbols from $\Sigma$. The empty string is denoted by $\varepsilon$, with length $\vert \varepsilon \vert = \mathbf{0}$.
- **$\Sigma^{*}$**: Set of *all* strings over $\Sigma$ (including $\varepsilon$).
- **$\Sigma^{+}$**: Set of *all non-empty* strings ($\Sigma^{+} = \Sigma^{*} - \{\varepsilon\}$).
- **Language ($L$)**: Any subset of $\Sigma^{*}$, i.e., $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$.

---

## 1.2 Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard 5-Tuple)

A DFA is defined as a 5-tuple:
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$$

| Component | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $Q$ | A finite, non-empty set of **states**. |
| $\Sigma$ | A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (alphabet). |
| $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | **Transition function** (total, deterministic). |
| $q_{0} \in Q$ | The unique **start state**. |
| $F \subseteq Q$ | Set of **final/accepting states**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinism Rule:** For every state $q \in Q$ and every input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, **exactly one** transition $\delta(q, a)$ must be defined. This is the *totality* condition.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Vending Machine Analogy):** Think of a DFA as a coin-operated elevator. You press a button (input symbol), and the elevator **must** move to one specific floor. There is *no choice*, *no guessing*, and *no parallel universes*. If the elevator ends on the "Ground Floor" (final state) after processing all buttons, your ride is accepted; otherwise, it is rejected.

**Real-World Engineering Parallel:** A DFA is the mathematical model behind lexical analyzers (lexers) in compilers, hardware circuit verification, and pattern-matching in network intrusion-detection systems.

---

## 1.3 Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard 5-Tuple)

An NFA is also a 5-tuple:
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$$

with one critical difference in the transition function:
$$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$

Here, $2^{Q}$ denotes the **power set of $Q$** — i.e., the set of *all possible subsets* of $Q$. The NFA can therefore transition to **zero, one, or many** states on a single input symbol.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Forking Paths Analogy):** Picture a maze explorer. At any junction, the explorer can *clone* themselves and explore **every possible path simultaneously** when they read a symbol. The string is **accepted** if **at least one clone** ends up in a final state at the end of the input. If all clones end up in non-final states (or dead ends), the string is rejected.

**Key Distinction from DFA:**
- DFA: $\delta(q, a) =$ **one** state.
- NFA: $\delta(q, a) =$ **a set** of states (possibly empty, possibly many).

---

## 1.4 NFA with $\varepsilon$-Transitions (NFA-$\varepsilon$)

An NFA-$\varepsilon$ is an NFA that additionally permits transitions on the **empty string** $\varepsilon$ without consuming any input symbol:
$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** NFA-$\varepsilon$ is a *strictly more convenient* modeling tool, but **NOT more powerful** than a standard NFA. Any NFA-$\varepsilon$ can be algorithmically converted to an equivalent NFA (and hence to a DFA) that accepts the *same language*.

---

## 1.5 Acceptance of a String

A string $w = w_{1} w_{2} \dots w_{n}$ is **accepted** by an automaton $M$ if, starting from $q_{0}$ and processing each symbol in order, the machine eventually lands in **at least one** state belonging to $F$. The **language accepted** by $M$ is:
$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid \hat{\delta}(q_{0}, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing \,\}$$

where $\hat{\delta}$ is the **extended transition function** (defined formally in Section 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Extended Transition Function ($\hat{\delta}$)

The transition function $\delta$ acts on a *single* input symbol. To reason about *entire strings*, we recursively extend it to $\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow 2^{Q}$.

### Recursive Definition (KTU Standard)

**Base Case:**
$$\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$$

**Inductive Case:** For $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ and $a \in \Sigma$:
$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition of the Formula:** "First, compute every state reachable on $w$. Then, for each of those states, take one more step on $a$, and gather *all* the resulting states into one set." This is the heart of every NFA algorithm.

### Acceptance (Revisited)

A string $w$ is accepted by $M$ iff:
$$\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing$$

---

## 2.2 $\varepsilon$-Closure (Critical for NFA-$\varepsilon$)

The $\varepsilon$-closure of a state $q$, written $E\text{close}(q)$ or $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$, is the set of **all states reachable from $q$ using only $\varepsilon$-transitions** (including $q$ itself).

**Definition:**
$$E\text{close}(q) = \{\, p \in Q \mid p \text{ is reachable from } q \text{ via zero or more } \varepsilon\text{-edges} \,\}$$

For a *set* of states $S$:
$$E\text{close}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E\text{close}(q)$$

The extended transition function for an NFA-$\varepsilon$ becomes:
$$\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = E\text{close}(q)$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = E\text{close}\!\left( \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a) \right)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| \# | Concept | Formula / Rule | Scope |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | DFA Transition | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | DFA |
| 2 | NFA Transition | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | NFA |
| 3 | NFA-$\varepsilon$ Transition | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | NFA-$\varepsilon$ |
| 4 | Base Recursion | $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$ | All FA |
| 5 | Inductive Step | $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$ | All FA |
| 6 | Acceptance | $\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing$ | All FA |
| 7 | $\varepsilon$-Closure | $E\text{close}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E\text{close}(q)$ | NFA-$\varepsilon$ |
| 8 | Max DFA States | $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ (subset bound) | DFA from NFA |
| 9 | Empty NFA Transition | $\delta(q, a) = \varnothing$ means **dead/reject** | All FA |
| 10 | NFA $\subseteq$ DFA (Power) | Every NFA-accepted language is DFA-acceptable | Theorem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** NFAs are exponentially more *compact* than their equivalent DFAs (often by a factor of $2^{n}$ states). Engineers use NFAs to *design* and DFAs to *implement* — this is the standard pattern in tools like `lex`/`flex`.

---

## 2.4 The Central Equivalence Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Rabin–Scott, 1959):** A language $L$ is accepted by **some NFA** if and only if $L$ is accepted by **some DFA**.

**Proof Strategy (subset construction):**
1. Each DFA state corresponds to a *subset* of NFA states.
2. A DFA state $S$ is final iff $S \cap F_{NFA} \neq \varnothing$.
3. The DFA's start state is $\{q_{0}\}$ (or its $\varepsilon$-closure).
4. Transitions follow $\delta_{DFA}(S, a) = \bigcup_{p \in S} \delta_{NFA}(p, a)$.

This algorithm is called the **Subset Construction** (or *powerset construction*).

---

## 2.5 Dead / Trap / Unreachable States

| Concept | Definition | Impact on DFA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dead State** | A non-final state that *only* transitions to itself. | All strings reaching it are rejected. |
| **Trap State** | Synonym for dead state in KTU usage. | Equivalent to $\varnothing$-sink. |
| **Unreachable State** | State with no path from $q_{0}$. | Can be deleted without changing $L(M)$. |
| **Inaccessible State** | Path exists but is not in any minimal computation. | Removed during minimization. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always draw an explicit **dead state** (or "error" state) when constructing a DFA from an NFA. Missing transitions in an NFA-to-DFA conversion are the #1 cause of partial-mark deductions in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.6 Why Both Models Exist (Engineering Rationale)

- **NFA**: Easier to *design* (intuitive, compact, modular, often with $\varepsilon$-transitions).
- **DFA**: Easier to *simulate* in hardware/software (single next-state lookup, no backtracking).
- **Workflow:** *Problem $\rightarrow$ NFA design $\rightarrow$ Subset construction $\rightarrow$ DFA implementation.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: DFA Construction (Strings ending in `a`)

**Language:** $L = \{\, w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid w \text{ ends with the symbol } a \,\}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the States

We need to track *what was the last symbol read*. So we use two non-final "memory" states plus one final state:

- $q_{0}$: Start state — "no input seen yet" (or last symbol was `b`).
- $q_{1}$: Last symbol was `a` (accepting state).
- $q_{2}$: Dead state (safety trap, needed for totality).

> [!NOTE]
> **State Design Heuristic (KTU Examiner's Rule):** "One state per *distinct memory class*." For 'ends with $a$', only **one bit of memory** is needed: *was the last symbol $a$?*

### Step 2 — Write the Formal 5-Tuple

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{2}\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $q_{0} = q_{0}$
- $F = \{q_{1}\}$

### Step 3 — Build the Transition Table

| State | On $a$ | On $b$ | Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $\rightarrow q_{0}$ | $q_{1}$ | $q_{0}$ | Start |
| $q_{1}$ (final) | $q_{1}$ | $q_{0}$ | Accepting |
| $q_{2}$ (dead) | $q_{2}$ | $q_{2}$ | Trap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic check:** From $q_{0}$ on $a$, we move to $q_{1}$ (because the new last symbol is $a$). From $q_{0}$ on $b$, we *stay* at $q_{0}$ (because the last symbol is still $b$, not $a$). From $q_{1}$ on $a$, we stay at $q_{1}$. From $q_{1}$ on $b$, we go back to $q_{0}$ because the last symbol is no longer $a$. The dead state $q_{2}$ is unreachable here, so it can be omitted for this language — but the KTU rubric *rewards* total DFAs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: NFA Construction (Strings ending in `01`)

**Language:** $L = \{\, w \in \{0, 1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ ends with the substring } 01 \,\}$.

### Step 1 — Design the NFA (Intuitive "Guess" Approach)

The NFA "guesses" which `0` is the *first* `0` of the trailing `01` and then verifies.

- $q_{0}$: Start state. Loops on both `0` and `1` (reading the prefix).
- $q_{1}$: "I just guessed this `0` is the start of `01`."
- $q_{2}$: Accepting state (we saw a `1` after the guessed `0`).

### Step 2 — Formal 5-Tuple

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{2}\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_{0} = q_{0}$
- $F = \{q_{2}\}$

### Step 3 — Transition Function $\delta$

| State | On $0$ | On $1$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $q_{0}$ | $\{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$ | $\{q_{0}\}$ |
| $q_{1}$ | $\varnothing$ | $\{q_{2}\}$ |
| $q_{2}$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is a *true* NFA:** Look at $q_{0}$ on input $0$: the machine has *two* choices — either stay at $q_{0}$ (continue the prefix) *or* jump to $q_{1}$ (start guessing). A DFA cannot have this ambiguity. This is the structural essence of non-determinism.

### Step 4 — Trace Acceptance of $w = 1101$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, 1) = \{q_{0}\}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, 11) = \delta(q_{0}, 1) = \{q_{0}\}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, 110) = \delta(q_{0}, 0) = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{0}, 1101) = \delta(q_{0}, 1) \cup \delta(q_{1}, 1) = \{q_{0}\} \cup \{q_{2}\} = \{q_{0}, q_{2}\}$$

Final check: $\{q_{0}, q_{2}\} \cap F = \{q_{2}\} \neq \varnothing$. ✅ **Accepted**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: NFA → DFA Conversion (Subset Construction)

**Input:** The NFA for "ends in `01`" from Section 3.2.

**Output:** An equivalent DFA whose states are *subsets* of $\{q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{2}\}$.

### Step 1 — Initialize the Start State

$$S_{0} = \{q_{0}\}$$

### Step 2 — Iteratively Compute New Subsets

We apply $\delta_{DFA}(S, a) = \bigcup_{p \in S} \delta_{NFA}(p, a)$ for every *new* subset $S$ and every $a \in \Sigma$.

**From $S_{0} = \{q_{0}\}$:**

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{0}, 0) = \delta(q_{0}, 0) = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\} \; \triangleq \; S_{1}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{0}, 1) = \delta(q_{0}, 1) = \{q_{0}\} = S_{0}$$

**From $S_{1} = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$ (new state, process it):**

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{1}, 0) = \delta(q_{0}, 0) \cup \delta(q_{1}, 0) = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\} = S_{1}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{1}, 1) = \delta(q_{0}, 1) \cup \delta(q_{1}, 1) = \{q_{0}\} \cup \{q_{2}\} = \{q_{0}, q_{2}\} \; \triangleq \; S_{2}$$

**From $S_{2} = \{q_{0}, q_{2}\}$ (new state, process it):**

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{2}, 0) = \delta(q_{0}, 0) \cup \delta(q_{2}, 0) = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\} = S_{1}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(S_{2}, 1) = \delta(q_{0}, 1) \cup \delta(q_{2}, 1) = \{q_{0}\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_{0}\} = S_{0}$$

**No new states emerge** — the construction terminates.

### Step 3 — Identify Final States

A DFA subset-state is final iff it contains **at least one** NFA final state ($q_{2} \in F_{NFA}$).

| Subset | Contains $q_{2}$? | Final? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_{0} = \{q_{0}\}$ | No | No |
| $S_{1} = \{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$ | No | No |
| $S_{2} = \{q_{0}, q_{2}\}$ | **Yes** | **Yes** ✅ |

### Step 4 — Final Equivalent DFA Transition Table

| State | On $0$ | On $1$ | Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $\rightarrow S_{0}$ | $S_{1}$ | $S_{0}$ | Start |
| $S_{1}$ | $S_{1}$ | $S_{2}$ | Intermediate |
| $*S_{2}$ | $S_{1}$ | $S_{0}$ | **Final** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Observation:** The original NFA had 3 states; the equivalent DFA has 3 states too. In this example, there is **no state explosion**. However, in the worst case, an NFA with $n$ states can require up to $2^{n}$ DFA states — this is the well-known **exponential blowup** phenomenon frequently tested in KTU Module 1 / Module 2 questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DFA vs NFA

| Feature | DFA | NFA | NFA-$\varepsilon$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transitions per $(q, a)$ | Exactly 1 | 0, 1, or many | 0, 1, or many |
| $\varepsilon$-moves | ❌ No | ❌ No (standard NFA) | ✅ Yes |
| Backtracking needed | No | Often simulated via BFS/DFS | Often simulated via BFS/DFS |
| Compactness (design) | Verbose | Concise | Most concise |
| Implementation ease | Direct (lookup table) | Requires state-set tracking | Requires $\varepsilon$-closure |
| Power (Languages accepted) | Regular | Regular | Regular |
| Max states vs equivalent NFA | Up to $2^{n}$ | $n$ | $n$ |
| Used in KTU design problems | Final implementation | Mid-step / design | Optional design aid |

        (prefix consumption)
    end note

```

stateDiagram-v2

    S0 --> S1 : 0
    S1 --> S1 : 0
    S1 --> S2 : 1

    S2 --> S1 : 0
    S2 --> [*]
    note right of S0
        S0 = {q0}
        Start state
    end note

        Prefix read;
        last symbol was 0
    end note

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NFA to DFA Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement in English] --> B[Step 1: Identify Memory Classes]
    B --> C[Step 2: Design Compact NFA]
    C --> D{NFA uses epsilon moves?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute Epsilon Closures]
    D -- No --> F[Skip Closure Step]
    E --> G[Step 3: Initialize DFA Start as Subset]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 4: BFS over Subsets]
    H --> I[Step 5: For Each New Subset S and Input a]
    I --> J[Compute Union over all p in S of delta NFA]
    J --> K{New subset generated?}
    K -- Yes --> H
    K -- No --> L[Step 6: Mark Subset as Final if it intersects F NFA]
    L --> M[Step 7: Draw Final DFA Diagram]
    M --> N[Step 8: Optional Minimization via Table Filling]

```

## 4.5 Comparative DFA vs NFA State Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input String w] --> B{DFA or NFA?}
    B -- DFA --> C[Read symbol a]
    C --> D[delta q a = exactly one state]
    D --> E{Final state reached?}
    E -- Yes --> F[ACCEPT]
    E -- No --> G[Continue or REJECT]

    B -- NFA --> H[Read symbol a]
    H --> I[Compute all p in delta q a]
    I --> J[Clone execution for each p]
    J --> K{Any clone in Final?}
    K -- Yes --> F
    K -- No --> G

    classDef acceptStyle fill:#15803d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rejectStyle fill:#991b1b,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class G rejectStyle
```
