- ✅ **Yield of a Tree:** Concatenation of leaf labels from **left to right**; this is the string the tree represents.
- ✅ **LMD (Leftmost Derivation):** Replaces the leftmost non-terminal at each step.
- ✅ **RMD (Rightmost Derivation):** Replaces the rightmost non-terminal at each step.
- ✅ **Theorem:** Number of LMDs = Number of RMDs = Number of derivation trees (for any string).
- ✅ **Ambiguity:** Grammar $G$ is ambiguous iff $\exists w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ distinct parse trees.
- ✅ **Inherent Ambiguity:** A language $L$ is inherently ambiguous if **every** grammar generating $L$ is ambiguous.
- ✅ **Dangling-Else:** Classic ambiguity in `if-then-else` constructs; resolved via matched/unmatched statement grammars.
- ✅ **Operator Precedence Fix:** Introduce intermediate non-terminals ($E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T \times F \mid F$) to enforce that $\times$ binds tighter than $+$.
- ✅ **No Algorithm Exists:** Detecting ambiguity in an arbitrary CFG is an **undecidable** problem — examiners will not ask you to write an algorithm, only to demonstrate ambiguity on a **specific string**.
- ✅ **Real-World Use:** YACC/Bison resolve conflicts using **precedence** (`%left`, `%right`) and **associativity** declarations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Forms for CFGs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT524 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Forms for CFGs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Normal Forms?

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** can express the same language in infinitely many different ways. A **Normal Form** is a standardized, restricted shape to which every CFG (for a context-free language) can be mechanically reduced. Two of the most important normal forms are:

### A. Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):** A CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Chomsky Normal Form** if every production in $P$ is of one of the two forms:
> 1. $A \rightarrow BC$  (a single variable on LHS, exactly two variables on RHS)
> 2. $A \rightarrow a$  (a single variable on LHS, exactly one terminal on RHS)
>
> Additionally, $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted **only if** $\varepsilon \in L(G)$.

### B. Greibach Normal Form (GNF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greibach Normal Form (GNF):** A CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Greibach Normal Form** if every production in $P$ is of the form:
> $$A \rightarrow a\alpha$$
> where $a \in T$ is a single terminal and $\alpha \in V^{*}$ is a (possibly empty) string of variables. The string $\alpha$ cannot be empty when the right-hand side is just a terminal — it must be a *string of zero or more variables* following a single starting terminal.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **chef** with a recipe book in which some recipes are written in long, free-form English paragraphs, while others use shorthand like "2 tsp salt". Other chefs cannot reliably reproduce the dish because the format is inconsistent.

- **CNF** is like rewriting every recipe so that every line contains *exactly two ingredients* (a "compound" ingredient) or *one final spice* (terminal). It is the format a precise machine-kitchen robot (CYK parser) needs.
- **GNF** is like rewriting every recipe so that *the first thing on each line is always a final spice*, followed by any number of compound ingredients. It is the format a "predictive" robot (LL/recursive-descent parser) needs.

Both describe the **same dishes** (languages), just in clean, predictable shapes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bother with Normal Forms?**
> 1. They enable polynomial-time parsing algorithms like **CYK** (for CNF) and **predictive top-down parsing** (for GNF).
> 2. They simplify proofs of fundamental theorems (e.g., the **Pumping Lemma for CFLs**, the **decidability of $L = \emptyset$**).
> 3. They expose structural properties of CFGs used in **compiler design**, **XML schema validation**, and **bioinformatics** (RNA structure prediction).

---

## 1.3 Summary of Restrictions

| Property | CNF | GNF |
|---|---|---|
| RHS starts with | either a single variable-pair $(BC)$ or a single terminal $a$ | always a single terminal $a$ |
| Length of RHS | 1 (terminal) or 2 (variables) | arbitrary, but the first symbol must be a terminal |
| $\varepsilon$ allowed? | only $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (and only if $\varepsilon \in L$) | not allowed |
| Unit productions ($A \rightarrow B$) | not allowed | not allowed |
| Mixed RHS ($A \rightarrow aB$) | not allowed | not allowed (must be $a\alpha$ with $\alpha$ all variables) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Pipeline: From Arbitrary CFG to Normal Form

Conversion to either normal form requires **pre-processing** the grammar. The same pre-processing pipeline feeds both CNF and GNF conversion.

### Pipeline Steps (in strict order)

1. **Ensure Start Symbol $S$ is Non-Recurring**
   - Introduce a new start symbol $S'$.
   - Add $S' \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$.
   - This guarantees $S$ never appears on the RHS of any production (which is required for the GNF substitution algorithm).

2. **Remove $\varepsilon$-Productions (Null Productions)**
   - A production $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is nullable.
   - For every production containing $A$ on the RHS, create a new production with **each subset** of nullable variables removed.
   - Finally, delete all $\varepsilon$-productions *except* $S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

3. **Remove Unit Productions**
   - A unit production is of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where $A, B \in V$.
   - If $B \rightarrow \alpha$ is a non-unit production, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ (for all such $B$).
   - Delete all unit productions. Iterate until none remain.

4. **Remove Useless Symbols**
   - A symbol is **generating** if it derives some terminal string.
   - A symbol is **reachable** if it appears in some sentential form derivable from $S$.
   - First, keep only **generating** symbols. Then, from $S$, keep only **reachable** symbols.

5. **(CNF only) Terminal-Replacement Pass**
   - For every production $A \rightarrow x_1 x_2 \dots x_n$ with $n \geq 2$ and any $x_i \in T$:
     - Introduce a new variable $C_i$ with $C_i \rightarrow x_i$.
     - Replace $x_i$ with $C_i$ in the production.

6. **(CNF only) Binarization Pass**
   - For every production $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 \dots B_n$ with $n \geq 3$:
     - Replace with $A \rightarrow B_1 D_1$, $D_1 \rightarrow B_2 D_2$, …, $D_{n-2} \rightarrow B_{n-1} B_n$.
   - Now every RHS has length at most **2 variables**.

7. **(GNF only) Ordering & Substitution Pass**
   - Order variables $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ (with start symbol first).
   - For each $A_i$, eliminate productions of the form $A_i \rightarrow A_j \alpha$ where $j < i$ by substituting the RHS of $A_j$'s productions.
   - Eliminate left-recursion $A_i \rightarrow A_i \alpha$ using the standard algebraic trick (introduce a new variable $Z_i$).

---

## 2.2 Why Each Step Is Necessary

> [!NOTE]
> - **$\varepsilon$-removal** is required because CNF/GNF only permit $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
> - **Unit-production removal** is required because CNF requires RHS to be either two variables or a single terminal — a single variable is forbidden.
> - **Useless-symbol removal** is required because any non-generating or unreachable symbol is "garbage" and complicates proofs/algorithms.
> - **Binarization** is required because CNF forbids RHS of length $\geq 3$.
> - **Ordering & substitution** is required for GNF to ensure that after substitution, every RHS begins with a terminal.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Definition / Rule | Key Equation / Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CNF Form 1** | Binary branching | $A \rightarrow BC$ |
| 2 | **CNF Form 2** | Lexical yield | $A \rightarrow a$ |
| 3 | **GNF Form** | Terminal-first production | $A \rightarrow a\alpha,\ \alpha \in V^{*}$ |
| 4 | **Nullable Variable** | $A \Rightarrow^{*} \varepsilon$ | Recursive closure of $\varepsilon$-derivability |
| 5 | **Generating Variable** | $A \Rightarrow^{*} w,\ w \in T^{*}$ | Set built bottom-up |
| 6 | **Reachable Variable** | $S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha A \beta$ | Set built top-down from $S$ |
| 7 | **Binarization bound** | Max RHS length after step 6 | $\vert \alpha \vert \leq 2$ |
| 8 | **GNF first-symbol rule** | First symbol of RHS | $a \in T$ |
| 9 | **Left-recursion removal** | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \vert \beta$ | $A \rightarrow \beta Z,\ Z \rightarrow \alpha Z \vert \varepsilon$ (adapted for GNF) |
| 10 | **Time complexity** | CNF conversion | $O(\vert P \vert \cdot n)$ where $n$ is max RHS length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to remember GNF's first-symbol rule:** "*GNF begins with a Golden (terminal) nugget*." Every production starts with a terminal, never a variable.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Normal Form |
|---|---|
| **Compilers** | CYK parser (CNF), Predictive parsers (GNF) for syntax analysis |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Probabilistic CFG parsing in constituency treebanks |
| **Bioinformatics** | Stochastic CFG for RNA secondary structure prediction |
| **XML / DTD Validation** | Document type definitions in CNF for efficient validation |
| **Model Checking** | Pushdown systems analyzed via CNF reachability |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Conversion to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

### Given CFG

$$G: S \rightarrow aAb \mid b,\quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow bB \mid b$$

### Step 1 — Ensure $S$ is Non-Recurring

Since $S$ does not appear on any RHS, no new start symbol is needed.

### Step 2 — Remove $\varepsilon$-Productions

There are no $\varepsilon$-productions in this grammar. **No change.**

### Step 3 — Remove Unit Productions

There are no unit productions. **No change.**

### Step 4 — Remove Useless Symbols

All variables are generating and reachable. **No change.**

### Step 5 — Terminal Replacement Pass

Introduce new variables to isolate terminals in mixed productions:

$$P_1 \rightarrow a,\quad P_2 \rightarrow b$$

Replace terminals in the productions with RHS of length $\geq 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_1 A P_2 \mid b \\
A &\rightarrow P_1 A \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Binarization Pass

The production $S \rightarrow P_1 A P_2$ has RHS of length 3. Introduce a new variable $A_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_1 A_3 \mid b \\
A_3 &\rightarrow A P_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final CNF Grammar

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_1 A_3 \mid b \\
A &\rightarrow P_1 A \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
P_1 &\rightarrow a \\
P_2 &\rightarrow b \\
A_3 &\rightarrow A P_2
\end{aligned}
}
$$

### Verification

| Production | Form | Valid CNF? |
|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow P_1 A_3$ | $A \rightarrow BC$ (two vars) | ✓ |
| $S \rightarrow b$ | $A \rightarrow a$ (one terminal) | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow P_1 A$ | $A \rightarrow BC$ | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow a$ | ✓ |
| $B \rightarrow bB$ | $A \rightarrow BC$ | ✓ |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | $A \rightarrow a$ | ✓ |
| $P_1 \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow a$ | ✓ |
| $P_2 \rightarrow b$ | $A \rightarrow a$ | ✓ |
| $A_3 \rightarrow A P_2$ | $A \rightarrow BC$ | ✓ |

All productions satisfy CNF. The string $aab$ is derived as:

$$
S \Rightarrow P_1 A_3 \Rightarrow a A_3 \Rightarrow a A P_2 \Rightarrow a P_1 A P_2 \Rightarrow a a A P_2 \Rightarrow a a a P_2 \Rightarrow a a a b
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Conversion to Greibach Normal Form (GNF)

### Given CFG (assumed already simplified to CNF form)

$$
S \rightarrow AB \mid a,\quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow b
$$

### Step 1 — Order Variables

Order: $A_1 = S,\ A_2 = A,\ A_3 = B$. (Start symbol first, then any consistent order.)

### Step 2 — Handle $A_3 = B$

The production $B \rightarrow b$ is already in GNF form ($A \rightarrow a\alpha$ with $\alpha = \varepsilon$, which is allowed since the first symbol is the terminal $b$). **No change.**

### Step 3 — Handle $A_2 = A$

The productions $A \rightarrow aA$ and $A \rightarrow a$ are already in GNF form. **No change.**

### Step 4 — Handle $A_1 = S$

The production $S \rightarrow AB$ has first symbol $A$ (a variable), not a terminal. Apply substitution.

Substitute the productions of $A$ (i.e., $aA$ and $a$) in place of $A$ on the RHS of $S \rightarrow AB$:

$$
S \rightarrow (aA)B \mid (a)B \mid a
$$

That is:

$$
S \rightarrow aAB \mid aB \mid a
$$

### Verification

| Production | First symbol is terminal? | Rest is variables? | Valid GNF? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow aAB$ | $a$ | $AB \in V^{*}$ | ✓ |
| $S \rightarrow aB$ | $a$ | $B \in V^{*}$ | ✓ |
| $S \rightarrow a$ | $a$ | $\varepsilon$ (allowed) | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow aA$ | $a$ | $A$ | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | $a$ | $\varepsilon$ | ✓ |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | $b$ | $\varepsilon$ | ✓ |

### Final GNF Grammar

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAB \mid aB \mid a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
}
$$

### Derivational Sanity Check

For the string $aab$:

$$
S \Rightarrow aAB \Rightarrow a aB \Rightarrow a a b \quad \checkmark
$$

For the string $ab$:

$$
S \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow a b \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 The General GNF Conversion Algorithm (Substitution Method)

Let the variables be ordered $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ with $A_1 = S$.

**For** $i = 1$ **to** $n$:
  **For** $j = 1$ **to** $i - 1$:
    **For** every production of the form $A_i \rightarrow A_j \gamma$:
      **For** every production $A_j \rightarrow \delta$:
        Add $A_i \rightarrow \delta \gamma$
      Delete $A_i \rightarrow A_j \gamma$
  **Remove any left-recursion** $A_i \rightarrow A_i \alpha \vert \beta$ using:
    Replace with $A_i \rightarrow \beta \vert \beta Z_i$ and $Z_i \rightarrow \alpha \vert \alpha Z_i$

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **high-level** schematic. KTU 2024 exam questions on GNF typically give a small CNF grammar (2–4 variables) and ask the student to perform the substitution. Memorize the **substitution rule** rather than the full algorithm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: CNF vs GNF Side-by-Side

| Property | CNF | GNF |
|---|---|---|
| Permitted production form | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ with $\alpha \in V^{*}$ |
| Max RHS length | 2 | unbounded |
| Derivation length for a string of length $n$ | exactly $2n - 1$ steps (balanced binary tree of derivations) | exactly $n$ steps (one per terminal) |
| Parsing algorithm enabled | CYK ($O(n^{3} \cdot \vert P \vert)$) | Predictive top-down (linear in $n$) |
| Used in | Membership testing, pumping lemma proofs | Recursive-descent compilers |
| Conversion difficulty | Moderate (mechanical binarization) | Higher (substitution + left-recursion elimination) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Pipeline: From Arbitrary CFG to Normal Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input CFG] --> stepB1[Ensure Start Symbol Non Recurring]
    stepB1 --> stepB2[Remove Epsilon Productions]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Remove Unit Productions]
    stepB3 --> stepB4[Remove Useless Symbols]
    stepB4 --> branchQ{Required Form?}
    branchQ -- CNF --> stepC1[Terminal Replacement Pass]
    stepC1 --> stepC2[Binarization Pass]
    stepC2 --> outCNF[Chomsky Normal Form Grammar]
    branchQ -- GNF --> stepD1[Order Variables]
    stepD1 --> stepD2[Substitution Pass]
    stepD2 --> stepD3[Left Recursion Elimination]
    stepD3 --> outGNF[Greibach Normal Form Grammar]
    outCNF --> endNode[Ready for Parsing / Proofs]
    outGNF --> endNode
```

## 4.2 CNF Conversion Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initial CFG] --> nodeB[Step 1: Introduce S Prime if S appears on RHS]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Find Nullable Variables and Remove Epsilon]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Identify Unit Productions A to B]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Compute Generating and Reachable Sets]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Replace Mixed Terminals with New Variables]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Binarize Productions with more than 2 Variables on RHS]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Final CNF Grammar]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Validation: Every Production is A to BC or A to a}
    nodeI -- Pass --> nodeJ[Done]
    nodeI -- Fail --> nodeK[Recheck Binarization]
    nodeK --> nodeG
```

## 4.3 GNF Conversion Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gA[Input Simplified CFG] --> gB[Order Variables A1 to An with S first]
    gB --> gC{For i from 1 to n}
    gC --> gD{For j from 1 to i minus 1}
    gD --> gE{Production Ai to Aj gamma exists}
    gE -- Yes --> gF[Substitute Aj productions in place of Aj]
    gF --> gG[Delete Ai to Aj gamma]
    gE -- No --> gH[Continue Loop]
    gG --> gH
    gH --> gI{Left recursion Ai to Ai alpha}
    gI -- Yes --> gJ[Apply Left Recursion Elimination Trick]
    gJ --> gK[Introduce New Variable Zi]
    gI -- No --> gL[Move to next i]
    gK --> gL
    gL --> gC
    gC -- Loop Done --> gM[Final GNF Grammar]
    gM --> gN{Validation: Every production starts with terminal}
    gN -- Pass --> gO[Done]
    gN -- Fail --> gP[Recheck Substitution]
    gP --> gB
```

## 4.4 CNF Derivation Tree Shape (for a string of length 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[S] --> nodeL1[A3]
    root1 --> nodeL2[b]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL3[A]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL4[B]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL5[a]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL6[b]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **derivation tree** for the string $ab$ under a hypothetical CNF grammar. Notice that:
> - Every **internal node** has **exactly two children** (binary tree property of CNF).
> - Every **leaf** is a terminal.
> - For a string of length $n$, the tree has exactly $2n - 1$ nodes (1 root + $n$ leaves + $n - 1$ internal binary-branching nodes).

## 4.5 GNF Derivation Tree Shape (for a string of length 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r1[S] --> ch1[a]
    r1 --> ch2[A1]
    ch2 --> ch3[b]
    ch2 --> ch4[A2]
    ch4 --> ch5[c]
    ch4 --> ch6[A3]
    ch6 --> ch7[d]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For a string of length $n$ in GNF, the derivation takes **exactly $n$ steps** because each step introduces exactly one terminal as the first symbol of the production. This is the key property that makes GNF grammars suitable for **predictive top-down parsing**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1: Define Chomsky Normal Form. State at least two conditions that every production in a CNF grammar must satisfy. Give one example of a grammar that is in CNF.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two forms:

1. $A \rightarrow BC$, where $A, B, C \in V$ (each of $B$ and $C$ is a variable, and the right-hand side has exactly two variables).
2. $A \rightarrow a$, where $A \in V$ and $a \in T$ (a single variable on LHS, exactly one terminal on RHS).

Additionally, the production $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted **only if** $\varepsilon \in L(G)$.

**Example of a CNF grammar:**

$$
S \rightarrow AB \mid a,\quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow bB \mid b
$$

Here:
- $S \rightarrow AB$ is of the form $A \rightarrow BC$.
- $S \rightarrow a$, $A \rightarrow a$, $B \rightarrow b$ are of the form $A \rightarrow a$.

All productions satisfy the CNF restrictions.

---

### **Question 2: Define Greibach Normal Form. Differentiate between CNF and GNF in terms of the structure of productions.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be in **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)** if every production is of the form:

$$A \rightarrow a\alpha$$

where $a \in T$ is a single terminal, and $\alpha \in V^{*}$ is a (possibly empty) string of variables.

**Differences between CNF and GNF:**

| Aspect | CNF | GNF |
|---|---|---|
| First symbol of RHS | Either a terminal (in $A \rightarrow a$) or a variable (in $A \rightarrow BC$) | **Always** a terminal |
| RHS length | At most 2 symbols | Arbitrary length |
| Form of $A \rightarrow$ | Two variables OR one terminal | One terminal followed by zero or more variables |
| $A \rightarrow BC$ allowed? | Yes | Not allowed (RHS starts with a variable) |
| $A \rightarrow aBC$ allowed? | Not allowed (RHS too long) | Yes (if $a \in T$ and $BC \in V^{*}$) |

The key distinction is that **CNF allows productions starting with a variable**, while **GNF requires every production to start with a terminal**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A: Convert the following CFG to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF). Show all intermediate steps.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]`

**Given CFG:**

$$S \rightarrow aAB \mid bC,\quad A \rightarrow bA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon,\quad C \rightarrow c$$

#### Part (a) — Pre-processing (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Ensure start symbol is non-recurring.**
$S$ does not appear on the RHS of any production. No new $S'$ required.

**Step 2: Remove $\varepsilon$-productions.**
The variable $B$ is nullable because $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

For every production containing $B$ on the RHS, add the version with $B$ removed:

- From $S \rightarrow aAB$: also add $S \rightarrow aA$ (removing $B$).
- From $B \rightarrow bB$: also add $B \rightarrow b$.

Delete $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$. Resulting grammar:

$$
S \rightarrow aAB \mid aA \mid bC,\quad A \rightarrow bA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow bB \mid b,\quad C \rightarrow c
$$

**Step 3: Remove unit productions.**
There are no unit productions ($A \rightarrow B$ form). **No change.**

**Step 4: Remove useless symbols.**
All variables $S, A, B, C$ are generating and reachable. **No change.**

#### Part (b) — CNF-specific passes (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Terminal replacement pass.**
Introduce new variables for terminals in mixed productions:

$$P_a \rightarrow a,\quad P_b \rightarrow b,\quad P_c \rightarrow c$$

Replace terminals in productions with RHS length $\geq 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_a A B \mid aA \mid bC \\
A &\rightarrow P_b A \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
C &\rightarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Binarization pass.**
Production $S \rightarrow P_a A B$ has length 3. Introduce $A_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_a A_3 \mid aA \mid bC \\
A_3 &\rightarrow A B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final CNF Grammar:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow P_a A_3 \mid aA \mid bC \\
A &\rightarrow P_b A \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
C &\rightarrow c \\
P_a &\rightarrow a,\quad P_b \rightarrow b,\quad P_c \rightarrow c \\
A_3 &\rightarrow A B
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### **Question B: Convert the following CNF grammar to Greibach Normal Form (GNF). Show all intermediate steps.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | 14 Marks]`

**Given CNF Grammar:**

$$S \rightarrow AB \mid a,\quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a,\quad B \rightarrow b$$

#### Part (a) — Ordering and substitution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Order the variables.**

Let $A_1 = S,\ A_2 = A,\ A_3 = B$. (Start symbol first.)

**Step 2: Process $A_3 = B$.**
The production $B \rightarrow b$ is already in GNF form. **No change.**

**Step 3: Process $A_2 = A$.**
The productions $A \rightarrow aA$ and $A \rightarrow a$ are already in GNF form (first symbol is the terminal $a$, rest is a variable string). **No change.**

**Step 4: Process $A_1 = S$.**
Production $S \rightarrow AB$ has first symbol $A$ (a variable). We substitute the productions of $A$:

For $A$, the productions are $A \rightarrow aA$ and $A \rightarrow a$.

Replace $A$ on the RHS of $S \rightarrow AB$ with these:

$$
S \rightarrow (aA)B \mid (a)B = aAB \mid aB
$$

The production $S \rightarrow a$ is already in GNF form.

#### Part (b) — Final verification and grammar (7 Marks)

**Final GNF Grammar:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAB \mid aB \mid a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Verification of GNF property:**

| Production | First symbol of RHS | Rest of RHS | In GNF form? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow aAB$ | $a$ (terminal) | $AB$ (variables) | ✓ |
| $S \rightarrow aB$ | $a$ (terminal) | $B$ (variable) | ✓ |
| $S \rightarrow a$ | $a$ (terminal) | $\varepsilon$ (allowed) | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow aA$ | $a$ (terminal) | $A$ (variable) | ✓ |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | $a$ (terminal) | $\varepsilon$ | ✓ |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | $b$ (terminal) | $\varepsilon$ | ✓ |

**Sample derivation (string $aab$):**

$$
S \Rightarrow aAB \Rightarrow a a B \Rightarrow a a b \quad \checkmark
$$

**Sample derivation (string $ab$):**

$$
S \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow a b \quad \checkmark
$$

All productions begin with a terminal, followed by zero or more variables — the grammar is in **GNF**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on):**
>
> 1. **Skipping the pre-processing steps.** Going directly to binarization without removing $\varepsilon$- or unit-productions first. **Loss: 3–4 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to handle the start symbol $S$.** If $S$ appears on the RHS of some production (e.g., due to a unit production $A \rightarrow S$ that was created), the resulting grammar may generate the wrong language. **Always introduce a new $S'$.**
>
> 3. **In $\varepsilon$-removal, missing the "subset" of nullable variables.** When $A \rightarrow BCDE$ and $B, C, D, E$ are all nullable, you must add productions for **every subset** of $\{B, C, D, E\}$ removed, not just one or two. There are $2^{4} = 16$ subsets. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **In CNF binarization, breaking the variable chain incorrectly.** When $A \rightarrow BCDE$ becomes $A \rightarrow BX_1$, $X_1 \rightarrow CX_2$, $X_2 \rightarrow DE$ — students often write $A \rightarrow BC$ and $C \rightarrow DE$ which is **wrong** and changes the language.
>
> 5. **In GNF substitution, leaving the production in the wrong form.** After substitution, students forget to delete the original $A_i \rightarrow A_j \gamma$ production, resulting in duplicate or wrong productions.
>
> 6. **Not verifying the final grammar is in the requested normal form.** Always do a final check: every production in CNF is of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$; every production in GNF starts with a terminal.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Normal Forms for CFGs):**
>
> - **CNF Definition:** Productions are of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (and possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$).
> - **GNF Definition:** Productions are of the form $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $a \in T$ and $\alpha \in V^{*}$.
> - **Pre-processing pipeline (apply in this order):**
>   1. New $S'$ if needed.
>   2. Remove $\varepsilon$-productions (carefully enumerate all subsets).
>   3. Remove unit productions (iterate until none remain).
>   4. Remove useless symbols (generating first, then reachable).
> - **CNF-specific:**
>   - Terminal-replacement pass: introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$ for every terminal $a$ appearing in a mixed production.
>   - Binarization pass: every RHS of length $\geq 3$ must be split into a chain of binary productions.
>   - Result: every RHS has length **at most 2**.
> - **GNF-specific:**
>   - Order variables: $S$ first, then any consistent order.
>   - Substitute productions of earlier variables into RHS of later variables.
>   - Eliminate left-recursion using the standard trick.
>   - Result: every RHS **starts with a terminal**.
> - **Quick checks:**
>   - CNF tree for a string of length $n$ has **exactly $2n - 1$ nodes**.
>   - GNF derivation for a string of length $n$ takes **exactly $n$ steps**.
> - **Why they matter:** CNF enables **CYK parsing** ($O(n^{3})$); GNF enables **predictive top-down parsing** (linear time).
> - **Languages with no GNF:** Only the language $\{\varepsilon\}$ (since GNF has no $\varepsilon$-productions, $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ alone doesn't fit the form).
> - **Pitfall:** Never apply binarization/substitution **before** removing $\varepsilon$- and unit-productions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Pushdown Automata (PDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT524 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Pushdown Automata (PDA)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Nondeterministic PDA (NPDA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board Examiner's Wording)**
> A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a finite-state machine equipped with an auxiliary **unbounded stack** as its working memory. When the transition function is allowed to produce **more than one possible next move** for a given configuration, the device is called a **Nondeterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA)**.

Formally, an NPDA is defined as the **7-tuple**:

$$
M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ F)
$$

where every component has a precise mathematical role:

| # | Component | Mathematical Role | Cardinality Constraint |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $Q$ | Finite set of internal **states** | $\vert Q \vert$ is finite |
| 2 | $\Sigma$ | Finite **input alphabet** (tape symbols) | $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is finite |
| 3 | $\Gamma$ | Finite **stack alphabet** | $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is finite |
| 4 | $\delta$ | **Transition function** | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ |
| 5 | $q_0$ | **Start state** | $q_0 \in Q$ |
| 6 | $Z_0$ | **Initial stack symbol** (bottom-of-stack marker) | $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ |
| 7 | $F$ | Set of **final (accepting) states** | $F \subseteq Q$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ codomain?**
> The codomain is the **power set** of $Q \times \Gamma^*$ because the NPDA may choose **any one of several** possible next moves. This is precisely what makes the automaton *nondeterministic* — the transition is a *relation*, not a *function*. A DPDA restricts this set to be a singleton (or empty).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Blindfold Maze Explorer

Imagine a person wearing a **blindfold** at the entrance of a **branching maze**, holding a **trail of pebbles in their left hand** (this is the *stack*). At every step they:
1. **Feel** the symbol written on the current cell of the maze (read an input symbol).
2. **Look at** the pebble currently on top of their hand (read the stack-top).
3. **Throw the pebble back, replace it, or add a new one** (push/pop/replace).
4. **Choose any one of the available tunnels** to walk into next (nondeterministic choice).

The explorer *accepts* the maze if they can reach a marked exit hall (**final state**) OR if they can empty their hand completely (**empty stack**). The trail of pebbles is essential — without it, the explorer could not remember how deep they went, and they could not recognise patterns like "$a^nb^n$" or palindromes.

> [!TIP]
> **Stack Memory is the Sole Source of Power Beyond Finite Control.** A finite automaton with the same number of states as the PDA *cannot* recognise context-free languages. It is the **unbounded, LIFO-accessible memory** that elevates the PDA to a CFL acceptor.

## 1.3 Why Nondeterminism is Required (and not a "Bug")

For certain Context-Free Languages such as $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ (even-length palindromes over $\{a,b\}$), there is **no DPDA** that accepts $L$. The automaton must *guess* where the middle of the string is — and that guess requires branching. NPDAs accept **exactly the class of Context-Free Languages**, while DPDAs accept only the **deterministic CFLs (DCFLs)**, a strict subset.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Transition

A transition of the form

$$
\delta(q,\ a,\ X) = \{(p_1,\ \gamma_1),\ (p_2,\ \gamma_2),\ \dots,\ (p_k,\ \gamma_k)\}
$$

must be interpreted as follows:

- The machine is currently in **state** $q$.
- It reads the input symbol $a$ (or chooses $\varepsilon$ for a *spontaneous move*).
- The top of the stack is $X \in \Gamma$.
- The machine **may** (nondeterministically) replace $X$ with any string $\gamma_i \in \Gamma^*$ and move to state $p_i$.

### Special Stack Operations Derived from the Replacement

| Intended Action | $\gamma$ chosen | Stack Effect |
|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Pop** $X$ | $\varepsilon$ | $X$ is removed |
| **No-op** | $X$ | Stack unchanged |
| **Push** $Y$ | $YX$ | $Y$ is added on top |
| **Replace** $X$ by $Y$ | $Y$ | $X$ is swapped for $Y$ |

## 2.2 Configuration (Instantaneous Description, ID)

The complete state of the NPDA at any instant is the triple:

$$
\text{ID} = (q,\ w,\ \gamma) \quad \text{where } q \in Q,\ w \in \Sigma^*,\ \gamma \in \Gamma^*
$$

- $q$ — current state
- $w$ — the **remaining** unread input suffix
- $\gamma$ — current **stack contents**, written with the top on the **left** (i.e., the top of the stack is the leftmost symbol)

**Initial ID:** $\ (q_0,\ w,\ Z_0)$

## 2.3 The Move (Turnstile) Relation

A single computational step is denoted by $\vdash$. Two flavours exist:

**1. Consuming-move** (uses input symbol $a \in \Sigma$):

$$
(q,\ a w',\ X \gamma) \ \vdash\ (p,\ w',\ \alpha \gamma) \quad \text{iff} \quad (p,\ \alpha) \in \delta(q,\ a,\ X)
$$

**2. $\varepsilon$-move** (no input consumed):

$$
(q,\ w',\ X \gamma) \ \vdash\ (p,\ w',\ \alpha \gamma) \quad \text{iff} \quad (p,\ \alpha) \in \delta(q,\ \varepsilon,\ X)
$$

The reflexive-transitive closure is written $\vdash^{*}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement | KTU Board-Exam Cue |
|---|---------|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **NPDA definition** | $M = (Q,\Sigma,\Gamma,\delta,q_0,Z_0,F)$ | Always write the 7-tuple explicitly. |
| 2 | **Transition map** | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ | Codomain is the **power set**. |
| 3 | **Initial configuration** | $(q_0,\ w,\ Z_0)$ | $Z_0$ sits at the **bottom** of the stack. |
| 4 | **Acceptance by Final State** | $w \in L(M) \iff \exists q \in F,\ \gamma \in \Gamma^* : (q_0,w,Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q,\varepsilon,\gamma)$ | Input exhausted **and** state is accepting. |
| 5 | **Acceptance by Empty Stack** | $w \in L(M) \iff \exists q \in Q : (q_0,w,Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q,\varepsilon,\varepsilon)$ | Input exhausted **and** stack is empty. |
| 6 | **Closure of $\vdash$** | $\vdash \subseteq \text{Conf}(M) \times \text{Conf}(M)$ | Reflexive-transitive closure is $\vdash^{*}$. |
| 7 | **NPDA $\Leftrightarrow$ CFG** | $L$ is context-free $\iff \exists$ NPDA $M$ with $L = L(M)$ | Always cite this in design proofs. |
| 8 | **NPDA $\Rightarrow$ DPDA inclusion** | $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | NPDAs are **strictly more powerful** than DPDAs. |
| 9 | **Length of computation** | For input $w$ with $\vert w \vert = n$, computation depth can be $O(n \cdot k)$ where $k$ is max stack push length. | Branches in nondeterministic trace may be exponential. |
| 10 | **Chomsky hierarchy slot** | NPDA $\to$ Type-2 (Context-Sensitive is more powerful) | NPDAs sit at the **CFL** tier. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of PDA / NPDA Concept |
|--------|---------------------------|
| **Compiler Design** | Recursive-descent and $\mathrm{LR}(1)$ parsers are essentially *deterministic* PDAs that recognise programming-language syntax (a CFL). |
| **XML / JSON Parsing** | Nested-tag languages are inherently context-free; PDA stack mirrors the nesting depth. |
| **Model Checking** | Pushdown systems model recursive programs; reachability is decidable via NPDAs. |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Nondeterminism captures ambiguity in human-language parse trees. |
| **Network Protocol Verification** | Recursive procedure calls modelled as pushdown systems verified by NPDA-equivalent machinery. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: NPDA for $L = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$

This is the **canonical** non-deterministic PDA problem. The language consists of all even-length palindromes over $\{a,b\}$. We will derive the NPDA in three stages.

### Stage 1 — Design Intuition

| Phase | NPDA Task | Stack Operation |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Phase 1 (state $q_0$)** | Read $w$, pushing every symbol onto the stack | **Push** |
| **Phase 2 ($\varepsilon$-move)** | *Guess* the middle of the string | **No change** |
| **Phase 3 (state $q_1$)** | Read $w^R$, matching and popping | **Pop** if top matches input |
| **Phase 4 (state $q_f$)** | When input is exhausted **and** only $Z_0$ remains | $\varepsilon$-move to final |

### Stage 2 — Formal Transition Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) = \{(q_0,\ a Z_0)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ a,\ a)   = \{(q_0,\ a a)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ a,\ b)   = \{(q_0,\ a b)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ b,\ Z_0) = \{(q_0,\ b Z_0)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ b,\ a)   = \{(q_0,\ b a)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ b,\ b)   = \{(q_0,\ b b)\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ X) = \{(q_1,\ X)\} \quad \text{for every } X \in \Gamma \\
&\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a)   = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b)   = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) = \{(q_f,\ Z_0)\} \\
&F = \{q_f\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transition $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, X) = \{(q_1, X)\}$ is the **nondeterministic guess**. The machine *guesses* the middle of the string at any point. Some guesses will lead to dead branches; the NPDA accepts iff **at least one branch** leads to acceptance.

### Stage 3 — Exhaustive Trace for $w = abba$

We follow the **accepting branch** of the nondeterministic trace. The stack is shown with its **top on the left** (K.T. convention).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 0:} \quad (q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) \\
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \vdash\ (q_0,\ bba,\ a Z_0) \quad \text{[read `a', push `a' on top of } Z_0] \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \vdash\ (q_0,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) \quad \text{[read `b', push `b' on top of `a']} \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \vdash\ (q_1,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) \quad \text{[\varepsilon-move: GUESS middle after `ab']} \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \vdash\ (q_1,\ a,\ a Z_0) \quad \text{[read `b', top is `b' \Rightarrow pop]} \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \vdash\ (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \quad \text{[read `a', top is `a' \Rightarrow pop]} \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \vdash\ (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \quad \text{[\varepsilon-move: ACCEPT]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the input is exhausted ($w = \varepsilon$) and the final state $q_f \in F$ is reached, the string $abba$ is **accepted**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Nondeterministic Trace Branches (the *Choice Tree*)

A KTU examiner will award **full marks only** if the student demonstrates awareness of the branching nature. Below is the **computation tree** for $abba$ (showing why *some* branches die but at least one survives).

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bba,\ aZ_0) \vdash (q_0,\ ba,\ baZ_0) \\
&\quad \big\downarrow \ \text{(Guess middle here — accepting branch)} \\
&\quad (q_1,\ ba,\ baZ_0) \vdash (q_1,\ a,\ aZ_0) \vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \vdash (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \\
&\quad \text{... but another branch would guess the middle TOO EARLY:} \\
(q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bba,\ aZ_0) \vdash (q_1,\ bba,\ aZ_0) \\
&\quad \text{[tries to pop `b' on top `a' \Rightarrow NO transition \Rightarrow dead branch]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The phrase "an NPDA accepts a string $w$" means $\exists$ (exists) at least one accepting path.** It does **not** mean all paths accept. This is the *existential* nature of nondeterminism.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NPDA vs DPDA vs FA

| Feature | Finite Automaton (FA) | Deterministic PDA (DPDA) | Nondeterministic PDA (NPDA) |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Auxiliary memory** | None | One unbounded LIFO stack | One unbounded LIFO stack |
| **Transition output** | A single next state | A single next state + stack update | A **set** of possible moves |
| **Language class accepted** | Regular | Deterministic CFL (DCFL) | All Context-Free Languages |
| **Power rank** | Lowest | Intermediate | Higher than DPDA |
| **Ambiguity handling** | N/A | Cannot handle inherent ambiguity | Can explore all derivations |
| **Decision procedure** | Polynomial | Polynomial | PSPACE-complete in general |
| **Example language accepted** | $(a+b)^*$ | $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 1\}$ | $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ |
| **Can accept $L = \{ww^R\}$?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

### 3.4 Theorem — NPDA $\Leftrightarrow$ Context-Free Grammar (Proof Outline)

**Theorem.** *A language $L$ is context-free if and only if there exists an NPDA $M$ such that $L = L(M)$.*

**Proof Sketch ($\Rightarrow$ direction — CFG to NPDA).**
Given $G = (V, T, P, S)$, construct $M = (\{q\}, T, V \cup T, \delta, q, S, \emptyset)$ with the two-rule template:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(a) For each production } A \to \alpha \in P: \quad \delta(q,\ \varepsilon,\ A) \ni (q,\ \alpha) \\
&\text{(b) For each terminal } a \in T: \quad \delta(q,\ a,\ a) = \{(q,\ \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rule (a) **expands** a non-terminal on the stack top (akin to leftmost derivation). Rule (b) **consumes** a terminal when it matches the stack top. By induction on the length of the derivation, $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$ in $G$ iff $(q,w,S) \vdash^{*} (q,\varepsilon,\varepsilon)$ in $M$.

**Proof Sketch ($\Leftarrow$ direction — NPDA to CFG).**
Given $M = (Q,\Sigma,\Gamma,\delta,q_0,Z_0,F)$, construct $G = (V,\Sigma,P,S)$ where the non-terminals are of the form $[pXq]$ meaning *“starting in state $p$ with stack-top $X$, the sub-machine can reach state $q$ while consuming some input and emptying $X$ from the stack.”* The productions are constructed from the transition rules, mirroring them. The proof proceeds by induction on the height of the parse tree. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NPDA

The figure below shows the **physical architecture** of an NPDA — its input tape, finite control unit, and the auxiliary LIFO stack — with the data-flow arrows that govern how each component interacts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    TAPE["INPUT TAPE: a1 a2 a3 ... an dollarsign"] -->|next symbol| HEAD["READ HEAD"]
    HEAD -->|a in Sigma or epsilon| FC["FINITE CONTROL Q q0 ... qk"]
    FC -->|push, pop, replace| STACKOP["STACK OPERATION UNIT"]
    STACKOP -->|manipulates| STOP["STACK TOP symbol in Gamma"]
    STOP --> STACKBODY["STACK BODY gamma1 gamma2 ..."]
    STACKBODY --> SBOT["STACK BOTTOM Z0"]
    STOP -->|top symbol exposed| FC
    FC -->|advance / hold| HEAD
    FC -->|next state p| FC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **stack top is exposed to the finite control**, and only the top symbol can be read or modified. This is the *LIFO access constraint* that distinguishes a PDA from a Turing machine (which has random access to the tape).

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram of the NPDA for $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$

The diagram below shows the three logical states of the worked example from Section 3. Self-loops on $q_0$ represent the push phase; the dashed transition represents the **nondeterministic guess**; transitions on $q_1$ represent the pop-and-match phase; the final transition enters the accepting state.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0((q0 push)) -- "a, Z to aZ" --> S0
    S0 -- "a, a to aa" --> S0
    S0 -- "a, b to ab" --> S0
    S0 -- "b, Z to bZ" --> S0
    S0 -- "b, a to ba" --> S0
    S0 -- "b, b to bb" --> S0
    S0 -. "eps, X to X  GUESS" .-> S1((q1 match))
    S1 -- "a, a to eps" --> S1
    S1 -- "b, b to eps" --> S1
    S1 -- "eps, Z to Z ACCEPT" --> SF((qf final))
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the dashed arrow matters in KTU answers:** the dashed transition from $q_0$ to $q_1$ is the *only* place where nondeterminism is exercised. Examiners explicitly look for the label $\varepsilon, X / X$ on this edge. Without it, the answer loses at least 2 marks.

## 4.3 Computation Tree for the Trace of $abba$ (Accepting Path Highlighted)

The diagram below visualises the **branching nondeterministic computation tree** for the input $abba$. Branches that fail are pruned, and the surviving accepting path is the certificate of acceptance.

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0["ID 0  q0 abba  Z0"] --> N1["ID 1  q0 bba  aZ0"]
    N1 --> N2["ID 2  q0 ba  baZ0"]
    N2 -->|GUESS 1| N3A["ID 3a  q1 ba  baZ0"]
    N2 -->|GUESS 2 DEAD| N3B["ID 3b  q1 bba  aZ0  no rule"]
    N3A --> N4["ID 4  q1 a  aZ0"]
    N4 --> N5["ID 5  q1 eps  Z0"]
    N5 --> N6["ID 6  qf eps  Z0  ACCEPT"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence vs. universality:** the NPDA *accepts* $abba$ because the path $N_0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_{3a} \to N_4 \to N_5 \to N_6$ reaches $q_f$. The dead branch $N_{3b}$ does **not** invalidate acceptance. This is the existential nature of nondeterministic acceptance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define a Nondeterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An NPDA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q,\Sigma,\Gamma,\delta,q_0,Z_0,F)$ where $Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\Gamma$ is the stack alphabet, $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ is the transition function mapping each (state, input-or-$\varepsilon$, stack-top) triple to a **finite set** of (state, stack-replacement) pairs, $q_0 \in Q$ is the start state, $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ is the initial stack symbol, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of final states. The codomain being a *power set* is what makes the device **nondeterministic** — for a given configuration, the machine may choose any of several successors. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Distinguish between a DPDA and an NPDA. Give one language accepted by an NPDA that no DPDA can accept.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **DPDA** has a transition function whose output is at most one move per configuration (i.e., the codomain is a singleton subset of $Q \times \Gamma^*$), whereas an **NPDA** may have multiple moves for the same configuration. DPDAs accept exactly the **deterministic context-free languages (DCFLs)**, while NPDAs accept the entire class of **context-free languages (CFLs)**. A canonical example of a CFL not in any DCFL is $L = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ — even-length palindromes over $\{a,b\}$ — which can be accepted by an NPDA (by guessing the middle) but **cannot** be accepted by any DPDA. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with an internal **OR** choice between two full questions.

---

### **Question 1 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Define a Pushdown Automaton formally. With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Nondeterministic Pushdown Automaton that accepts the language

$$
L = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}
$$

by final state. Show the **complete accepting trace** for the input string $abba$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q1(a) — 7 marks

A PDA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ with the following components:

| Component | Role | Marks |
|-----------|------|-------|
| $Q$ | Finite set of control states | 1 |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | 0.5 |
| $\Gamma$ | Stack alphabet | 0.5 |
| $\delta$ | Transition function $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ | 2 |
| $q_0$ | Start state | 0.5 |
| $Z_0$ | Initial stack symbol (bottom-of-stack) | 0.5 |
| $F$ | Set of accepting states | 0.5 |
| Block diagram | Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes | 1.5 |

The **finite control** is the brain; the **input tape** is read-only and one-way; the **stack** provides unbounded LIFO memory that distinguishes a PDA from a finite automaton. The transition function being *relation-valued* (codomain a power set) is the hallmark of nondeterminism.

#### Model Solution to Q1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Specify the 7-tuple** (2 marks):

$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_f\},\ \{a, b\},\ \{a, b, Z_0\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ \{q_f\})
$$

**Step 2 — Define the transition function** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\delta(q_0,\ a,\ X) = \{(q_0,\ aX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ b,\ X) = \{(q_0,\ bX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ X) = \{(q_1,\ X)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a) = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b) = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) = \{(q_f,\ Z_0)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Trace for $w = abba$** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bba,\ aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ ba,\ baZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ ba,\ baZ_0) \quad \text{[NONDET GUESS]} \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ a,\ aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \\
&\vdash (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \quad \text{[ACCEPT]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Input exhausted and $q_f \in F$ — **string accepted**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question 2 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** State and explain the equivalence between NPDAs and Context-Free Grammars (CFGs). Outline the proof that every CFL has an equivalent NPDA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct an NPDA that accepts the language

$$
L = \{a^n b^m a^n b^m \mid n, m \ge 1\}
$$

by empty stack. Show the trace for the input $aabbaabb$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q2(a) — 7 marks

**Theorem.** *A language $L$ is context-free if and only if there exists an NPDA $M$ with $L = L(M)$.* **(1 mark)**

**Forward direction (CFG $\Rightarrow$ NPDA):** Given $G = (V, T, P, S)$, construct the single-state NPDA

$$
M = (\{q\},\ T,\ V \cup T,\ \delta,\ q,\ S,\ \emptyset)
$$

with two rule types **(3 marks)**:
- For every production $A \to \alpha \in P$: $\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) \ni (q, \alpha)$ — *expand* a non-terminal on the stack top.
- For every terminal $a \in T$: $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ — *consume* a matching terminal.

By induction on the length of the derivation, $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$ in $G$ iff $(q, w, S) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ in $M$. Hence $L(G) = N(M)$ (empty-stack acceptance). **(2 marks)**

**Reverse direction (NPDA $\Rightarrow$ CFG):** Construct non-terminals of the form $[pXq]$ meaning *"from state $p$ with $X$ on top, the sub-machine reaches state $q$ and pops $X$"*. Translate each transition $\delta(p, a, X) \ni (q_1, Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k)$ into productions. By induction on the height of the parse tree, $L(G) = L(M)$.

#### Model Solution to Q2(b) — 7 marks

**Design Intuition.** The string $a^n b^m a^n b^m$ has the structure $w_1 w_2$ where $w_1 = a^n b^m$ and $w_2 = w_1^R$ over the "alphabet" $\{a, b\}$ with the constraint that the middle swaps $a \leftrightarrow b$. A two-phase NPDA suffices.

**NPDA Definition** (3 marks):

$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_f\},\ \{a, b\},\ \{a, b, Z_0\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ \emptyset)
$$

where (accepting by empty stack):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\delta(q_0,\ a,\ X) = \{(q_0,\ aX)\} \quad \forall X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ b,\ X) = \{(q_0,\ bX)\} \quad \forall X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ X) = \{(q_1,\ X)\} \quad \forall X \in \{a, b, Z_0\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a) = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b) = \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
&\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) = \{(q_f,\ \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for $aabbaabb$** (4 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aabbaabb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ abbaabb,\ aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bbaabb,\ aaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ baabb,\ baaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ aabb,\ bbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ abb,\ abbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bb,\ aabbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ b,\ baabbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ bbaabbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ bbaabbaaZ_0) \quad \text{[GUESS middle]} \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ baabbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ aabbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ abbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ bbaaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ baaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ aaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \\
&\vdash (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon) \quad \text{[EMPTY-STACK ACCEPT]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Input exhausted and stack is empty — **accepted**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where B.Tech Students Lose Marks on PDA Questions (Decoded from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 7-tuple** — A 7-mark "design" question without an explicit 7-tuple is capped at **5/7**. Always begin the design with $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$.
> 2. **Wrong stack-top convention** — KTU uses **top-on-the-left**. Writing the stack as $Z_0 a b a$ when the top is $a$ costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing push with replace** — Rule $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(q, a)\}$ is a **replace** ($X$ removed, $a$ added), not a push. Use $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(q, aX)\}$ to *push*.
> 4. **Not labelling the $\varepsilon$-transition** — The "guess the middle" move is the *defining* nondeterministic step. Omitting its label forfeits at least **2 marks**.
> 5. **Showing only the accepting branch** without acknowledging that *other branches die* — for full credit, mention that "the NPDA accepts iff at least one branch leads to a final state / empty stack."
> 6. **Mixing acceptance criteria** — If the question says "by final state," the stack need **not** be empty at acceptance; if it says "by empty stack," the state need **not** be in $F$. Confusing the two is a fatal error.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for NPDA (Module 3)**
> - ✅ **7-tuple:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ — always write explicitly.
> - ✅ **Transition map codomain:** $2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ — the *power set* is the source of nondeterminism.
> - ✅ **Configuration (ID):** $(q, w, \gamma)$ with **stack top on the LEFT**.
> - ✅ **Move notation:** $\vdash$ for one step, $\vdash^{*}$ for closure.
> - ✅ **Two acceptance criteria:** (i) final state, (ii) empty stack — they are **equivalent** for NPDAs (with a small construction).
> - ✅ **Initial ID:** $(q_0, w, Z_0)$.
> - ✅ **Pop operation:** replace top $X$ with $\varepsilon$.
> - ✅ **Push operation:** replace top $X$ with $YX$ (new symbol $Y$ on top).
> - ✅ **$\varepsilon$-move:** allows a state/stack change *without* consuming input — essential for nondeterministic guessing.
> - ✅ **NPDA accepts exactly the CFLs** — this is the central theorem of Module 3.
> - ✅ **NPDA $\supsetneq$ DPDA** in power: $L = \{ww^R\}$ is the standard witness.
> - ✅ **Always cite the trace IDs** in ID form: $(q, w, \gamma)$ — KTU valuation keys expect this notation.
> - ✅ **Nondeterministic acceptance = existential:** "$\exists$ an accepting path," *not* "all paths accept."
> - ✅ **Stack symbol $Z_0$** is the bottom-of-stack marker; it must be pushed initially and must remain at the bottom.
> - ✅ **In trace answers, write the input *remaining***, not the part already consumed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT524 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)

A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a 7-tuple formal machine used to recognize context-free languages. It extends the finite automaton by adding a **single, unbounded stack** as auxiliary storage.

A PDA is mathematically defined as the **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

Where the components are defined as:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $Q$ | A finite, non-empty set of **states** |
| $\Sigma$ | A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (input alphabet) |
| $\Gamma$ | A finite, non-empty set of **stack symbols** (stack alphabet) |
| $\delta$ | The **transition function** |
| $q_0 \in Q$ | The designated **start state** |
| $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ | The **initial stack symbol** (placed on stack at the start) |
| $F \subseteq Q$ | The set of **accepting / final states** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The transition function $\delta$ is the heart of the PDA. For a non-deterministic PDA, $\delta$ is a mapping $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to \text{finite subsets of } Q \times \Gamma^*$. The **non-determinism** here is what separates a general PDA from a **DPDA**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA)

A **Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA)** is a restricted PDA in which **no configuration has more than one legal next move**. The transition function is single-valued (returns either exactly one move or none).

For a DPDA, the transition function is:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma^* \cup \{\emptyset\}$$

Subject to the following **two mandatory determinism conditions** for every $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$, and $X \in \Gamma$:

1. **Condition 1 (No $\varepsilon$-conflict):** If $\delta(q, a, X) \neq \emptyset$ for some input symbol $a$, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \emptyset$.  
   *(If a move on an input symbol exists, no $\varepsilon$-move is allowed on the same state-symbol pair.)*

2. **Condition 2 (Uniqueness of move):** For any $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, if $\delta(q, a, X) = (p, \gamma)$, then for any other $a' \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ and any other state $p'$, we must have $\delta(q, a', X) = (p', \gamma')$ only if $a \neq a'$ or $p = p'$.  
   *(In simple terms: at most one move is possible at every step.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A DPDA is a PDA in which for each configuration $(q, w, \gamma)$ there is **at most one** next configuration. Equivalently, the transition function is defined only for **specific** triples and never returns a set with more than one element.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **clerk standing at a warehouse loading dock**:
- The clerk can read a label (input symbol).
- The clerk has a single vertical **stack of crates** (the stack) where he can only push (add) or pop (remove) from the top.
- The clerk must always know **exactly what to do next** based on three things: his current task, the label he is reading, and the top crate visible.

**Non-deterministic PDA (NPDA):** The clerk, upon seeing a label, can do several different things at the same time — push crate A, push crate B, or pop the top crate. He explores all possibilities in parallel (like multiple universes).

**Deterministic PDA (DPDA):** The clerk follows a **strict rulebook** — for every (current task, label, top crate) combination, the rulebook specifies **at most one action**. There is no ambiguity; the clerk follows a single, predictable path.

The **determinism** makes the DPDA a practical model for real compilers and parsers (e.g., **LR-parsers** are essentially DPDAs in disguise), while NPDA is more powerful but non-implementable in a single thread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** DCFL (Deterministic Context-Free Languages) is the class of languages accepted by DPDAs. It is a **proper subset** of CFL (Context-Free Languages) — meaning there exist CFLs that **no** DPDA can accept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a DPDA Transition

A transition of a DPDA is written in the form:

$$\delta(q, a, X) = (p, \gamma)$$

The interpretation is:
- The machine is in state $q$.
- It reads input symbol $a$ (where $a$ can be an input symbol or $\varepsilon$ for a stack-only move).
- The top of the stack is $X$.
- It **pops** $X$ from the stack, **pushes** the string $\gamma$ onto the stack (leftmost symbol of $\gamma$ becomes the new top), and transitions to state $p$.

If $a = \varepsilon$, the transition is called an **$\varepsilon$-transition** or **$\lambda$-move** — the input is not consumed.

### 2.2 Acceptance Modes in DPDA

A DPDA can accept a string in two different ways. Unlike NPDA, in DPDA these two modes are **not equivalent**.

#### Mode 1: Acceptance by Final State
The DPDA halts and is in a state $q \in F$ (final state) after consuming the **entire input string**. The stack may be non-empty.

$$w \in L(M) \iff (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \gamma) \text{ for some } q \in F \text{ and } \gamma \in \Gamma^*$$

#### Mode 2: Acceptance by Empty Stack
The DPDA halts after consuming the entire input string, and the **stack is completely empty** (regardless of the state).

$$w \in L(M) \iff (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) \text{ for some } q \in Q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** In a **general PDA**, acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack are **equivalent** (you can always convert one form to the other). In a **DPDA**, this equivalence **breaks down** — there exist DCFLs accepted by final state that **cannot** be accepted by empty stack and vice versa.
> - To make them equivalent, the DPDA must satisfy a **normal form** condition: no $\varepsilon$-transitions from a final state, and the stack is never empty when a final state is entered (one must explicitly add an "emptying phase").

### 2.3 The Class DCFL — Deterministic Context-Free Languages

**DCFL** is the class of all languages accepted by some DPDA. Formally:

$$L \in \text{DCFL} \iff \exists \text{ DPDA } M \text{ such that } L = L(M)$$

#### Key Properties of DCFL:
- **DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL** (strict subset — not all CFLs are deterministic).
- **DCFL is closed under complement.** If $L \in \text{DCFL}$, then $\overline{L} \in \text{DCFL}$.
- **DCFL is closed under inverse homomorphisms.**
- **DCFL is NOT closed under union, intersection, concatenation, or Kleene star.** (These closures hold for CFL but fail for DCFL.)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | KTU Board Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PDA tuple** | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | Definition question (2 marks) |
| **DPDA transition** | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma^*$ | Definition & construction (5+ marks) |
| **NPDA transition** | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ | Comparison question |
| **Determinism Condition 1** | $\delta(q, a, X) \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \emptyset$ | Proof / justification questions |
| **Final State Acceptance** | $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), q \in F\}$ | Standard DPDA acceptance |
| **Empty Stack Acceptance** | $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$ | Conversion question |
| **Language Class** | $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | Hierarchy of language classes |
| **DCFL closure** | Closed under $\cap^c$ (complement) only | 14-mark proof question |
| **DCFL non-closure** | Not closed under $\cup, \cap, \cdot, {}^*$ | Counter-example question |
| **Inherent Ambiguity** | A CFL is *inherently ambiguous* if no unambiguous grammar exists for it; such languages are **not in DCFL** | 7-mark proof question |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DPDA is the **theoretical foundation of modern compiler design**:
- **LR(1), LALR(1) parsers** used in tools like YACC, Bison, ANTLR are direct implementations of DPDAs.
- The class **DCFL** corresponds exactly to languages generated by **LR(k) grammars** — the most powerful class of deterministic grammars used in practice.
- Every **deterministic programming language syntax** (C, Java, Python grammar restricted to its unambiguous subset) lies in DCFL.
- DPDA theory is also used in **XML schema validation**, **protocol verification**, and **digital circuit testing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Takeaway:** Whenever you write a parser in a compiler course, you are essentially **constructing a DPDA** by hand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: PDA vs. NPDA vs. DPDA

| Property | General PDA (NPDA) | DPDA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transition type | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to (Q \times \Gamma^*) \cup \{\emptyset\}$ |
| Number of next moves | 0, 1, or many | 0 or exactly 1 |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Allowed in isolation | Only when no input-consuming move is defined |
| Language class accepted | CFL | DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL |
| Final state vs. empty stack | Equivalent | **NOT** equivalent in general |
| Practical use | Theoretical (non-implementable directly) | Compiler parsers, LR(1) |
| Closure under $\cup$ | Yes | **No** |
| Closure under $\cap$ | No | **No** |
| Closure under complement ($\cap^c$) | No | **Yes** |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Proof: Why is the language $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ NOT in DCFL?

This is a classic KTU question. We prove that no DPDA can accept $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ (the language of even-length strings where the second half equals the first half).

#### Step 1: Assume for contradiction that there exists a DPDA $M$ that accepts $L$.

Suppose $M$ has $k$ states. The DPDA must, after reading the first half of a string, somehow "remember" it on the stack, then compare it to the second half. Since $M$ is deterministic, its behavior on the second half is a **function** of its current state and the top of the stack.

#### Step 2: Construct a "pumping-style" adversarial argument.

Choose any word $w$ such that $ww \in L$ and $|w| \geq k$ (long enough that the DPDA must have repeated some internal stack-and-state configuration during the first half).

There exist non-empty strings $u, v, x$ with $w = uvx$ and $\vert v \vert \geq 1$ such that during the processing of $w$, the DPDA enters the same (state, stack-top) configuration after reading $u$ and after reading $uv$. This is the **pumping property** of CFLs.

#### Step 3: Exploit the determinism.

Because $M$ is deterministic, whatever happens after reading $u$ also happens after reading $uv$. The DPDA cannot "branch" or "look ahead" — its future is locked by the configuration.

So for any integer $i \geq 0$, the string $uv^i x w$ is also accepted by $M$. But when $i$ becomes very large, the "first half" of $uv^i x w$ becomes longer than the "second half" $w$, breaking the structure of $L$.

#### Step 4: Formalize the contradiction.

Specifically, choose $i$ such that $\vert uv^i x \vert > \vert w \vert$. The string $uv^i x w$ has length $\geq 2 \cdot \vert uv^i x \vert$, so it cannot be of the form $zz$ unless the "first half" (which is $uv^i x$) equals the "second half" (which is $w$).

But $\vert uv^i x \vert \neq \vert w \vert$ by our choice, so $uv^i x w \notin L$. This contradicts our assumption that $M$ accepts $L$.

#### Step 5: Conclusion.

$$\therefore \text{No DPDA can accept } L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$$
$$\therefore L \notin \text{DCFL} \quad \text{(even though } L \in \text{CFL)}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction: DPDA for $L = \{wcw^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$

The language $L = \{wcw^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ consists of all even-length palindromes with a **middle marker $c$**. This is the classic "deterministic palindrome" — the marker $c$ tells the DPDA when to switch from "pushing" to "matching/popping".

#### DPDA Construction:

Let $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$
- $\Gamma = \{Z_0, A, B\}$ (with $Z_0$ as initial stack symbol)
- $F = \{q_2\}$

#### Transition Function $\delta$ (table form):

| Current State | Input Symbol | Stack Top | Next State | Stack Action | Meaning |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $Z_0$ | $q_0$ | $AZ_0$ | Push $A$ for $a$ |
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $A$ | $q_0$ | $AA$ | Push $A$ for $a$ |
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $B$ | $q_0$ | $AB$ | Push $A$ for $a$ |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $Z_0$ | $q_0$ | $BZ_0$ | Push $B$ for $b$ |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $A$ | $q_0$ | $BA$ | Push $B$ for $b$ |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $B$ | $q_0$ | $BB$ | Push $B$ for $b$ |
| $q_0$ | $c$ | $Z_0$ | $q_1$ | $Z_0$ | See marker, switch to match mode |
| $q_0$ | $c$ | $A$ | $q_1$ | $A$ | See marker, switch to match mode |
| $q_0$ | $c$ | $B$ | $q_1$ | $B$ | See marker, switch to match mode |
| $q_1$ | $a$ | $A$ | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | Match $a$ by popping $A$ |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | $B$ | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | Match $b$ by popping $B$ |
| $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $q_2$ | $Z_0$ | Stack contains only $Z_0$ — accept |

#### Trace of the DPDA on input $abcba$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(q_0, abcba, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, bcba, AZ_0) \quad [\text{read } a, \text{ push } A] \\
&\vdash (q_0, cba, BAZ_0) \quad [\text{read } b, \text{ push } B] \\
&\vdash (q_1, ba, BAZ_0) \quad [\text{read } c, \text{ switch to match mode}] \\
&\vdash (q_1, a, AZ_0) \quad [\text{read } b, \text{ pop } B] \\
&\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \quad [\text{read } a, \text{ pop } A] \\
&\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0) \quad [\varepsilon\text{-move, accept}]
\end{aligned}$$

The DPDA **accepts** $abcba \in L$ by final state. ✓

#### Why this is deterministic:

For every $(q, a, X)$ triple, there is **at most one** transition rule defined. There is no conflict between $\varepsilon$-moves and input-consuming moves (e.g., $\delta(q_1, a, A) = (q_1, \varepsilon)$ is defined, but $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, A) = \emptyset$ — so no conflict).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The marker symbol $c$ is the **key to determinism** for palindromes. Without the marker, the language $ww^R$ is still in CFL (accepted by NPDA) but **not in DCFL**, because the DPDA has no way to know "when to start matching" without a signal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Architecture of a DPDA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputTape["INPUT TAPE\nContains input string w\nRead head moves LEFT to RIGHT"] --> ControlUnit
    ControlUnit["CONTROL UNIT\nHolds current state q\nExecutes transition function delta"] 
    ControlUnit --> StackMechanism
    StackMechanism["STACK MECHANISM\nUnbounded LIFO stack\nTop symbol X is read\nPush or pop operation"]
    StackMechanism --> ControlUnit
    ControlUnit -- "Next state p" --> OutputDecision{"Is q in F?\nAND input consumed?"}
    OutputDecision -- Yes --> Accept["ACCEPT w"]
    OutputDecision -- No --> Reject["REJECT / HALT"]
```

### 4.2 State Transition Flow for the DPDA on $L = \{wcw^R\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : Read a or b\nPush A or B\nonto stack
    q0 --> q1 : Read marker c\nKeep stack\nunchanged
    q1 --> q1 : Read a with A on top\nPop A\nOR Read b with B on top\nPop B
    q1 --> q2 : Epsilon move\nStack contains only Z0
    q2 --> [*] : ACCEPT\nFinal state reached
    q1 --> Reject : Mismatch\n(input symbol does\nnot match stack top)
    Reject --> [*] : REJECT
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: NPDA vs DPDA Branching

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Configuration\nq, w, X"] --> Decision1{"Determinism\nCondition Check"}
    Decision1 -- "DPDA:\nAt most 1 move" --> Single["Single Next\nConfiguration\np1, w1, gamma1"]
    Decision1 -- "NPDA:\nMultiple moves\npossible" --> Multi1["Branch 1:\np1, w1, gamma1"]
    Decision1 -- "NPDA:\nMultiple moves\npossible" --> Multi2["Branch 2:\np2, w2, gamma2"]
    Decision1 -- "NPDA:\nMultiple moves\npossible" --> MultiN["Branch N:\npN, wN, gammaN"]
    Single --> DoneDPDA["Process Continues\nDeterministically"]
    Multi1 --> DoneNPDA["Parallel Exploration\nAccept if ANY branch\nreaches final state"]
    Multi2 --> DoneNPDA
    MultiN --> DoneNPDA
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Is a Language $L$ in DCFL?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given a language L"] --> Q2{"Is L context-free?\nDoes a CFG exist?"}
    Q2 -- No --> Result1["L is NOT in CFL\nL is NOT in DCFL"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{"Does a DPDA exist\nthat accepts L?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Result2["L is in DCFL\nL is in CFL\nL is accepted by LR k parser"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is L inherently\nambiguous?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Result3["L is NOT in DCFL\nL is in CFL but not DCFL"]
    Q4 -- No --> Result4["L may or may not be in DCFL\nTry harder construction"]
```

### 4.5 Hierarchy of Language Classes (KTU 2024 Module 3 Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Regular["REGULAR LANGUAGES\nAccepted by DFA / NFA\nGenerated by Regular Grammar\nClosed under: union, complement,\nintersection, concatenation, star"]
    DCFL["DCFL\nAccepted by DPDA\nGenerated by LR k grammar\nClosed under: complement only\nnot union, not intersection"]
    CFL["CFL\nAccepted by PDA\nGenerated by CFG\nClosed under: union, concatenation\nNOT closed under intersection or\ncomplement"]
    CSL["CONTEXT-SENSITIVE LANGUAGES\nAccepted by LBA\nGenerated by CSG"]
    Recursive["RECURSIVE LANGUAGES\nDecidable by Turing Machine\nTotal functions"]
    RE["RE LANGUAGES\nAccepted by Turing Machine\nSemi-decidable"]
    Regular --> DCFL
    DCFL --> CFL
    CFL --> CSL
    CSL --> Recursive
    Recursive --> RE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Insight:** The strict subset $\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ is a **classic KTU 2-mark question**. The inclusions are strict: there exist DCFLs that are not regular (e.g., $L = \{a^n b^n\}$), and CFLs that are not in DCFL (e.g., $L = \{ww^R\}$, the palindrome language).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA). State the two conditions that must be satisfied by the transition function of a DPDA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA)** is a restricted form of PDA in which every configuration has at most one next move. Formally, it is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where the transition function is:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to (Q \times \Gamma^*) \cup \{\emptyset\}$$

The **two conditions** for determinism are:
1. For any $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$, and $X \in \Gamma$: if $\delta(q, a, X) \neq \emptyset$ then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \emptyset$. (No conflict between input-consuming and $\varepsilon$-moves.)
2. For any $q \in Q$ and $X \in \Gamma$, the values $\delta(q, a, X)$ for different $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ are either empty or return disjoint next configurations. (At most one move per step.) **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE. Justify. "The set of languages accepted by DPDAs is closed under union."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**FALSE.** The class DCFL is **not closed under union**.

**Justification by counter-example:** Consider two DCFLs:
- $L_1 = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ — accepted by a DPDA.
- $L_2 = \{b^n a^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ — accepted by a DPDA.

Their union $L_1 \cup L_2 = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\} \cup \{b^n a^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is in CFL (an NPDA can nondeterministically guess which form the input takes). However, **no DPDA can accept $L_1 \cup L_2$**, because the DPDA must commit to one of the two strategies (push $a$'s or push $b$'s) on the very first symbol it reads, and this commitment is irreversible.

Hence, DCFL is not closed under union. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (DPDA Questions):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Writing the transition function with `|` for sets like $\{(p, \gamma)\}$ inside derivations — KTU examiners want set notation correctly typed or explicitly listed.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to **prove determinism** when constructing a DPDA. Simply listing transitions is not enough — explicitly verify Conditions 1 and 2.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing **acceptance by final state** with **acceptance by empty stack** in DPDA proofs. These are **NOT equivalent** for DPDA — a 2-mark loss trap.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to state the **initial stack symbol $Z_0$** explicitly. This is a free mark and easy to miss.
> - **Common mistake 5:** For counter-examples showing DCFL is not closed under an operation, students often give a *regular* language as the counter-example — this does not work because DCFL *contains* regular languages. The counter-example must use **two non-regular DCFLs** or show that the result is **inherently ambiguous**.

---

#### Part B — Option A (14 Marks)

**Q. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Define a DPDA formally. Explain how it differs from a general (non-deterministic) PDA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a DPDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ by final state. Show the trace of your DPDA on the input $aabbcc$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **DPDA** is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where all components are as in PDA, but the transition function is:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to (Q \times \Gamma^*) \cup \{\emptyset\}$$

The two **determinism conditions** are:
1. If $\delta(q, a, X)$ is defined for some $a \in \Sigma$, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is **undefined**.
2. For every $q \in Q$, $X \in \Gamma$, and $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, at most one transition is defined.

**Differences from general PDA:**

| Aspect | General PDA | DPDA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transition output | Set of configurations | At most one configuration |
| Multiple $\varepsilon$-moves | Allowed | Not allowed simultaneously with input moves |
| Language class | CFL | DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL |

The key difference: an NPDA can branch into multiple computation paths and **accepts if any one accepts**. A DPDA has a **single computation path**, so accepting depends entirely on that one path. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**DPDA Construction for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$:**

Let $M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}, \{a,b,c\}, \{Z_0, A\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_3\})$.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

| State | Input | Stack Top | Next State | Stack Action | Purpose |
