# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a recursive language. How is it different from a recursively enumerable language?**

**Model Answer:**

A language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that **halts on every input** and accepts if $w \in L$, rejects if $w \notin L$.

A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable (r.e.)** if there exists a TM $M$ that halts and accepts when $w \in L$, but may **loop forever** when $w \notin L$.

**Key Difference:** Recursive requires guaranteed termination on **all** inputs; r.e. only requires acceptance of valid inputs. Every recursive language is r.e., but the converse is not true (e.g., $L_u$ is r.e. but not recursive).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State whether the class of recursively enumerable languages is closed under complement. Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

**No**, the class of r.e. languages is **not closed under complement**.

**Justification:** If r.e. were closed under complement, then for any r.e. language $L$, $\overline{L}$ would also be r.e. By Theorem 2, $L \in \mathcal{RE}$ and $\overline{L} \in \mathcal{RE}$ would imply $L$ is recursive. This would mean $\mathcal{RE} = \mathcal{R}$, contradicting the existence of $L_d$ (diagonalization language) which is r.e. but not recursive. Hence r.e. is **not** closed under complement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a) Define recursive and recursively enumerable languages. Prove that every recursive language is recursively enumerable. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Recursive Language:** $L$ is recursive if $\exists$ a decider TM $M$ that halts on every input $w \in \Sigma^*$, accepting if $w \in L$ and rejecting if $w \notin L$.
- **Recursively Enumerable Language:** $L$ is r.e. if $\exists$ a TM $M$ that halts and accepts whenever $w \in L$ (no guarantee of halting for $w \notin L$).

**Proof (5 marks):**

Let $L \in \mathcal{R}$. Then $\exists$ a decider $M$ for $L$ such that $M$ halts on every input.

Construct $M'$ that operates on input $w$:

$$M'(w): \quad \text{1. Run } M(w). \quad \text{2. If } M \text{ accepts, then } M' \text{ accepts. } \quad \text{3. If } M \text{ rejects, then } M' \text{ rejects.}$$

Since $M$ always halts, $M'$ also always halts. The behavior of $M'$:

- $w \in L \Rightarrow M$ halts in $q_a \Rightarrow M'$ halts in $q_a$ (accepts).
- $w \notin L \Rightarrow M$ halts in $q_r \Rightarrow M'$ halts in $q_r$ (rejects).

Thus $M'$ is a recognizer for $L$ (in fact, also a decider). Therefore $L \in \mathcal{RE}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Define the diagonalization language $L_d$. Show that $L_d$ is recursively enumerable but not recursive. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fix an enumeration of all TMs $\{M_1, M_2, M_3, \ldots\}$ and all strings $\{w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots\}$. The diagonalization language is:

$$L_d = \{ w_i \mid w_i \notin L(M_i) \}$$

**Proof that $L_d \in \mathcal{RE}$ (3 marks):**

Construct $M_{L_d}$:

$$M_{L_d}(w): \quad \text{1. Find index } i \text{ with } w = w_i. \quad \text{2. Simulate } M_i(w_i). \quad \text{3. If } M_i \text{ halts and rejects } w_i, \text{ accept.}$$

If $w = w_i \in L_d$, then $w_i \notin L(M_i)$, so $M_i$ never accepts. $M_{L_d}$ recognizes this case and halts with acceptance. Hence $L_d \in \mathcal{RE}$.

**Proof that $L_d \notin \mathcal{R}$ (3 marks):**

Assume for contradiction that $L_d \in \mathcal{R}$. Then $\exists$ decider $M_k$ such that $L(M_k) = L_d$.

Consider $w_k$:

- If $w_k \in L_d$: By definition, $w_k \notin L(M_k)$. But $M_k$ decides $L_d$, so $w_k \in L(M_k)$. **Contradiction.**
- If $w_k \notin L_d$: By definition, $w_k \in L(M_k)$. But $M_k$ decides $L_d$, so $w_k \in L_d$. **Contradiction.**

Both cases give contradictions, so $L_d \notin \mathcal{R}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q: (a) State and prove the theorem: A language $L$ is recursive if and only if both $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are recursively enumerable. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 mark):** $L \in \mathcal{R} \iff L \in \mathcal{RE} \text{ and } \overline{L} \in \mathcal{RE}$.

**Proof ($\Rightarrow$ direction, 3 marks):**

Suppose $L \in \mathcal{R}$. Then $\exists$ decider $M$ for $L$. By the result of Question A(a), $L \in \mathcal{RE}$. For $\overline{L}$, construct $M'$ by swapping $q_a$ and $q_r$ in $M$. Since $M$ halts on all inputs, $M'$ also halts on all inputs, and accepts exactly $\overline{L}$. Hence $\overline{L} \in \mathcal{R} \subseteq \mathcal{RE}$.

**Proof ($\Leftarrow$ direction, 3 marks):**

Suppose $L \in \mathcal{RE}$ and $\overline{L} \in \mathcal{RE}$. Let $M_1$ recognize $L$ and $M_2$ recognize $\overline{L}$. Construct decider $M$ using **dovetailing**:

$$M(w): \quad \text{1. Run one step of } M_1(w). \quad \text{2. Run one step of } M_2(w). \quad \text{3. If } M_1 \text{ accepts, } M \text{ accepts. } \quad \text{4. If } M_2 \text{ accepts, } M \text{ rejects. } \quad \text{5. Go to step 1.}$$

Since $w \in L$ xor $w \in \overline{L}$, at least one of $M_1$ or $M_2$ must accept eventually. $M$ always halts with a definitive answer. Hence $L \in \mathcal{R}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Define the Universal Language $L_u$. Show that $L_u$ is recursively enumerable but not recursive. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**

$$L_u = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a valid encoding of a TM, and } w \in L(M) \}$$

**$L_u$ is r.e. (3 marks):**

Construct the **Universal Turing Machine** $U$:

$$U(\langle M, w \rangle): \quad \text{1. Simulate } M \text{ on } w \text{ step by step.} \quad \text{2. If } M \text{ halts in } q_a, U \text{ accepts.} \quad \text{3. If } M \text{ halts in } q_r, U \text{ rejects (or loops).}$$

If $\langle M, w \rangle \in L_u$, then $w \in L(M)$, so $M$ halts and accepts, and $U$ halts and accepts. Thus $L_u \in \mathcal{RE}$.

**$L_u$ is not recursive (3 marks):**

Suppose decider $D$ for $L_u$ exists. Construct decider for $L_d$ as follows. For input $w$, find index $i$ with $w = w_i$, build TM $M_i'$ that simulates $M_i$ on $w_i$:

- If $M_i$ accepts $w_i$, then $M_i'$ rejects $w_i$.
- If $M_i$ rejects $w_i$, then $M_i'$ accepts $w_i$.

Then $w \in L_d \iff M_i'$ rejects $w_i \iff w_i \notin L(M_i')$. Now feed $\langle M_i', w_i \rangle$ to $D$:

- $D$ accepts $\Rightarrow \langle M_i', w_i \rangle \in L_u \Rightarrow w_i \in L(M_i') \Rightarrow w \notin L_d \Rightarrow$ reject.
- $D$ rejects $\Rightarrow \langle M_i', w_i \rangle \notin L_u \Rightarrow w_i \notin L(M_i') \Rightarrow w \in L_d \Rightarrow$ accept.

This gives a decider for $L_d$, contradicting $L_d \notin \mathcal{R}$. Hence $L_u \notin \mathcal{R}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "halts" with "accepts":** A TM halting in $q_r$ still halts — only looping is non-halting. Marks lost for sloppy language.
> 2. **Forgetting the strict containment:** Many students write $\mathcal{R} = \mathcal{RE}$. Always state $\mathcal{R} \subsetneq \mathcal{RE}$ with proof via $L_d$.
> 3. **Closure property errors:** r.e. is **NOT** closed under complement — this is the most commonly tested point. Always cite the diagonalization argument.
> 4. **Dovetailing description:** Students forget to mention that $M_1$ and $M_2$ run on **separate tapes** in parallel simulation; sequential running does not guarantee halting.
> 5. **Encoding notation:** $\langle M, w \rangle$ must be specified as the concatenation of the binary encoding of $M$ and $w$ — not just written as a pair.
> 6. **Skipping the contradiction step:** In $L_d$ proof, both cases (Case A and Case B) must be explicitly shown. Marks deducted if only one case is given.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages**

- **Recursive ($L \in \mathcal{R}$):** Decider TM exists, halts on **every** input, definitive yes/no answer.
- **r.e. ($L \in \mathcal{RE}$):** TM exists that halts and accepts valid inputs; may loop on invalid inputs.
- **Strict Containment:** $\mathcal{R} \subsetneq \mathcal{RE}$ — proven by diagonalization language $L_d$.
- **Diagonalization Language:** $L_d = \{w_i \mid w_i \notin L(M_i)\}$ — r.e. but not recursive.
- **Universal Language:** $L_u = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid w \in L(M)\}$ — r.e. but not recursive.
- **Halting Problem:** $H = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ — r.e. but not recursive.
- **Bridge Theorem:** $L \in \mathcal{R} \iff L \in \mathcal{RE} \text{ and } \overline{L} \in \mathcal{RE}$.
- **Dovetailing:** Technique to convert parallel recognizers into a single decider.
- **Closure Properties:** $\mathcal{R}$ closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal, homomorphism.
- **$\mathcal{RE}$ Closure:** Closed under union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal, homomorphism; **NOT closed under complement**.
- **TMs and Decidability:** Halting on every input = recursive; Halting on accepting inputs only = r.e.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Compiler optimization, antivirus detection, program verification, AI computability limits.
- **Two-Case Contradiction:** $L_d$ proof uses both $w_k \in L_d$ and $w_k \notin L_d$ to derive contradictions.
- **Reduction Strategy:** To show $L_u \notin \mathcal{R}$, reduce to $L_d$ via encoding and complement trick.
